content: Lions live in groups called prides, typically consisting of 10 to 15 individuals, sometimes up to 40. A pride usually contains 2-3 adult males, 5-10 related females, and their cubs. Lionesses are the primary hunters, working together using coordinated ambush strategies. Males protect the territory from rival males and predators like hyenas.

Lionesses hunt cooperatively, surrounding prey and driving it toward ambush positions. Lions can sprint at up to 80 kilometres per hour but only for 100-200 metres. They primarily hunt at night when their superior night vision gives them an advantage. Common prey includes zebra, wildebeest, buffalo, and antelope.

African lion populations have declined dramatically — from an estimated 200,000 a century ago to fewer than 25,000 today, an 80% decline. Major threats include habitat loss from farming, human-wildlife conflict, trophy hunting, and climate change reducing prey populations. Lions are listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List.

A lion's roar can be heard up to 8 kilometres away and serves to communicate territory boundaries and coordinate pride members. Lions also communicate through head rubbing, scent marking, facial expressions, and various vocalizations including grunts, purrs, and moans. Cubs learn to roar at about one year of age.

Lion cubs are born blind and helpless, weighing about 1.5 kilograms. Lionesses in a pride often give birth around the same time and communally nurse each other's cubs. Cubs begin hunting training at about 3 months through play-fighting and stalking games. Young males leave the pride at 2-3 years to find their own territory.

As apex predators, lions play a crucial role in maintaining ecosystem balance. By controlling herbivore populations, they prevent overgrazing and allow vegetation to regenerate. This cascading effect supports biodiversity throughout the savanna. When lion populations decline, the entire ecosystem can become unbalanced.

Male lions are distinguished by their manes, which darken with age and serve to protect the neck during fights and attract females. Adult males weigh 150-250 kg and measure 1.7-2.5 metres in body length. Lions are the second-largest living cats after tigers. They can sleep up to 20 hours a day to conserve energy.

A small population of Asiatic lions survives in the Gir Forest of Gujarat, India. With only about 700 individuals, they are critically important for genetic diversity. The Gir lions are slightly smaller than their African cousins and have a distinctive belly fold. India's conservation efforts have helped this population recover from fewer than 20 individuals in the early 1900s.
